coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots as metal-free nanozymes for chemodynamic therapy of cancer
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Hongji
Deng, Zhiming
Zhang, Zonghui
Lin, Wenchu
Zhang, Miqin
Wang, Hui
coredata.dc:description: Metal-based nanozymes have shown remarkable potential as therapeutic agents for cancer treatment. However, the issue of off-target side effects associated with metal toxicity remains a significant challenge. Here, we synthesized N/P co-doped graphene quantum dots (NPGQDs) as metal-free nanozymes for catalytic tumor therapy. NPGQDs were derived from erythrocyte membranes, ensuring the absence of organic precursors and solvents that could potentially contaminate the final product. NPGQDs efficiently converted hydrogen peroxide into hydroxyl radicals, leading to intracellular oxidative damage and inhibition of tumor cell proliferation. The exceptional peroxidase-mimetic enzymatic activity of NPGQDs was attributed to a synergistic electron effect resulting from co-doping N and P in GQDs. The in vivo experiments demonstrated that NPGQDs exhibited excellent biocompatibility and that the treatment with NPGQDs significantly suppressed tumor growth and prolonged survival without the need for therapeutic drugs. This drug-free, target-specific, and biologically benign nanozyme holds great potential as a potent biocatalyst for safe cancer treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: graphene quantum dots
metal-free nanozymes
diatomic doping
synergistic electron effect
tumor catalytic therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and structural characterizations of NPGQDs
In vitro induction of apoptosis and ferroptosis by NPGQDs
In vivo therapeutic assessment of NPGQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by financial support from the National Key R&D Program of China (grant no. 2021YFA1600202 ); the fellowship of the China National Postdoctoral Program for Innovative Talents ( BX20230111 ); the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( U2032162 ); the Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation ( 2308085J18 ); the Hefei Institutes of Physical Science Director’s Fund ( BJPY2021B06 ); the Collaborative Innovation Program of Hefei Science Center of CAS ( 2022HSC-CIP013 ); and the High Magnetic Field Laboratory of Anhui Province ( AHHM-FX-2021-04 ). A portion of this work was supported by the High Magnetic Field Laboratory of Anhui Province. The numerical calculations in this work were performed on the supercomputing system at the Supercomputing Center of the University of Science and Technology of China.

This work was funded by financial support from the National Key R&D Program of China (grant no. 2021YFA1600202); the fellowship of the China National Postdoctoral Program for Innovative Talents (BX20230111); the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U2032162); the Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation (2308085J18); the Hefei Institutes of Physical Science Director's Fund (BJPY2021B06); the Collaborative Innovation Program of Hefei Science Center of CAS (2022HSC-CIP013); and the High Magnetic Field Laboratory of Anhui Province (AHHM-FX-2021-04). A portion of this work was supported by the High Magnetic Field Laboratory of Anhui Province. The numerical calculations in this work were performed on the supercomputing system at the Supercomputing Center of the University of Science and Technology of China. Conceptualization, H.L. (lead), M.Z. (lead), and H.W. (lead); formal analysis, H.L. (lead), M.Z. (lead), and H.W. (lead); funding acquisition, H.L. (lead) and H.W. (lead); investigation, H.L. (lead), Z.D. (supporting), and Z.Z. (supporting); methodology, H.L. (lead) and H.W. (lead); validation, H.L. (lead) and Z.D. (supporting); visualization, H.L. (lead), M.Z. (lead), and H.W. (lead); writing – original draft, H.L. (lead), M.Z. (lead), and H.W. (lead); writing – review & editing, H.L. (lead), M.Z. (lead), and H.W. (lead); project administration, W.L. (equal), M.Z. (lead), and H.W. (lead); resources, W.L. (equal) and H.W. (lead); supervision, W.L. (equal), M.Z. (lead), and H.W. (lead), The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing the role of NPGQDs as POD-mimic enzymes for tumor catalytic therapy
The structure characterization of NPGQDs
(A) The synthesis process of NPGQDs through a diagram illustration.
(B) TEM image and optical photo (inset) of NPGQDs.
(C) HRTEM image of NPGQDs.
(D) HRTEM image of a single NPGQD with the red dotted curves encircling the vacancy defects.
(E) AFM image and the corresponding height profile of NPGQDs.
(F and G) Typical XRD pattern (F) and Raman spectrum (G) of NPGQDs.
The surface properties, electronic states, and optical performance of NPGQDs
(A) XPS survey of NPGQDs, with an inset showing the expanded plots of the binding energy between 138 and 128 eV.
(B–D) XANES spectra of NPGQDs presented for the C K-edge (B), N K-edge (C), and P L-edge (D).
(E) XPS scans of P2p.
(F) A typical EPR spectrum of NPGQDs.
(G) UV-vis absorption and excitation spectra of NPGQDs, with an inset showing the images of dispersed NPGQDs without/with UV light irradiation.
(H) The PL spectra of NPGQDs.
(I) Time-resolution PL spectrum of NPGQDs.
POD-like catalytic properties and DFT calculation
(A) Schematic illustration of the POD-like function of NPGQDs.
(B) EPR signals of DMPO-·OH under different treatments.

(C) Catalytic reaction velocity of NPGQDs as a function of H2O2 concentration, represented by Michaelis-Menten curves (n = 3), with an inset showing the chemical reaction of the POD-like activity of NPGQDs.
(D) Various doping states considered in the DFT calculation.
(E) Energy diagram illustrating the reaction processes involved in the POD-like activity.
Therapeutic properties of NPGQDs
(A) Viability of 4T1 cells treated with NPGQDs of various concentrations at pH 7.4 and 6.2 (n = 6).
(B) Fluorescence images of dead/live staining of 4T1 cells treated with NPGQDs of various concentrations at pH 6.2; scale bar: 200 μm.
(C) Fluorescence images of ROS staining of 4T1 cells incubated with varying concentrations of NPGQDs at pH 6.2; scale bar: 100 μm.
(D and E) Viability of 4T1 cells pre-incubated with (D) ascorbic acid or (E) α-tocopherol, followed by treatment with NPGQDs at 100 μg mL−1 under weak acidic conditions (pH 6.2) (n = 6).
(F) Viability of 4T1 cells pre-incubated with ferrostatin-1 or Ac-DEVD-CHO, followed by treatment with NPGQDs (100 μg mL−1) under acidic conditions (pH 6.2) (n = 6).
Therapeutic efficacy of NPGQDs in an aggressive mouse model of TNBC
(A) Treatment schedule illustrating the administration and therapeutic assessment of NPGQDs.
(B) Relative tumor volumes measured over a 15-day period in mice receiving different treatment options, normalized to the tumor volume before NPGQD injection (n = 5).
(C) Tumor volumes measured at day 15 from mice receiving different treatments (n = 5).
(D) Body weights of mice monitored over the 15-day period following NPGQD injection (n = 5).

(E and F) Photos (E) and measured weights (F) of tumors collected from mice at day 15 after receiving different treatments (n = 5). (I) PBS-treated group, (II) NPGQD (i.v.)-treated group, and (III) NPGQD (i.t.)-treated group.
(G) Survival curves of tumor-bearing mice receiving different treatments. Statistical analysis was performed using Student’s two-tailed t test (n = 5, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001).
Immunofluorescence staining analysis of tumor tissues
(A) H&E staining showing tumor tissue morphology.
(B) TUNEL staining indicating cell apoptosis (green) in tumor tissues.
(C) ROS staining demonstrating the presence of ROS (green) in tumor tissues.
(D and E) Quantification of TUNEL labeling intensity (D) and ROS intensity (E) based on analysis of 8 random fields of view selected from each treatment group.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots as metal-free nanozymes for chemodynamic therapy of cancer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: High Magnetic Field Laboratory, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, Anhui 230031, P.R. China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: High Magnetic Field Laboratory, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, Anhui 230031, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, Hunan 410082, P.R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, Hunan 410082, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Washington, Seattle, WA 98195, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Washington, Seattle, WA 98195, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-based nanozymes have shown remarkable potential as therapeutic agents for cancer treatment. However, the issue of off-target side effects associated with metal toxicity remains a significant challenge. Here, we synthesized N/P co-doped graphene quantum dots (NPGQDs) as metal-free nanozymes for catalytic tumor therapy. NPGQDs were derived from erythrocyte membranes, ensuring the absence of organic precursors and solvents that could potentially contaminate the final product. NPGQDs efficiently converted hydrogen peroxide into hydroxyl radicals, leading to intracellular oxidative damage and inhibition of tumor cell proliferation. The exceptional peroxidase-mimetic enzymatic activity of NPGQDs was attributed to a synergistic electron effect resulting from co-doping N and P in GQDs. The in vivo experiments demonstrated that NPGQDs exhibited excellent biocompatibility and that the treatment with NPGQDs significantly suppressed tumor growth and prolonged survival without the need for therapeutic drugs. This drug-free, target-specific, and biologically benign nanozyme holds great potential as a potent biocatalyst for safe cancer treatment.

The research explores graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as metal-free nanozymes for highly efficient tumor chemodynamic therapy (CDT). Although CDT has been well known by cancer-related practitioners, the potential safety of metal-based nanocatalytic reagents has yet to be explored, while the catalytic activity of non-metallic nanocatalytic drugs is insufficient in inhibiting tumor proliferation. This study introduces N, P diatomic doping as a means to adjust the catalytic performance of NPGQDs. The synergistic electron effect of N and P atoms dramatically reduced the energy barrier of the peroxidase-mimetic reaction, thus enhancing the enzyme activity. NPGQDs derived from green precursors avoid product contamination and harmful reagent use, thereby ensuring their excellent biological safety. Given excellent catalytic ability, high tumor specificity, and biological benignity, NPGQDs have achieved safe and efficient tumor treatment effects and represent the development direction of CDT.

Derived from erythrocyte membranes, the metal-free nanozymes, known as N/P co-doped graphene quantum dots (NPGQDs), demonstrate exceptional peroxidase-mimetic enzymatic activity. They effectively induce intracellular oxidative damage, impede tumor proliferation, and promote apoptosis and ferroptosis. In murine models, NPGQDs accumulate in tumors, significantly inhibiting tumor growth, improving survival rates, and providing a safe nanocatalyst for cancer treatment with valuable therapeutic implications.

ce:section.ce:para:
Chemodynamic therapy (CDT) is an emerging non-invasive and highly effective cancer treatment modality; the transition of CDT from lab investigation and theory to clinical practice is anticipated with great enthusiasm. 1
,
2
,
3 This treatment strategy harnesses the potential of nanozymes, which are drug-free entities, to catalyze the breakdown of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) presenting in cancer cells, leading to the production of the highly cytotoxic hydroxyl radical (·OH). 4
,
5
,
6 Nanozymes cause oxidative damage in cancer cells, obviating the need for aggressive chemo drugs or damaging radiation. 7
,
8
,
9
,
10 Notably, nanozymes can serve as triggers to regulate the production of ·OH after they are endocytosed by tumor cells characterized by weak acidity and a high concentration of H2O2. 11
,
12
,
13
,
14 However, the majority of nanozymes synthesized to date are derived from organic/inorganic precursors and solvents, leading to the inevitable generation of harmful by-products and environmental pollution. 15
,
16
,
17 Metal-based nanozymes, the most widely studied enzyme mimics, exhibit enzyme-like activities comparable to those of natural enzymes. 18
,
19
,
20
,
21 Nevertheless, the potential toxicity associated with these metal-based nanozymes remains a significant safety concern. 22
,
23
,
24 Thus, the development of a metal-free nanozyme, preferably derived from environmentally friendly synthesis methods, is critical for tumor-specific CDT.

Carbon-based nanomaterials have emerged as promising alternatives to metal-based nanozymes due to their inherent peroxidase (POD)-mimetic properties and excellent biocompatibility. 25
,
26
,
27 Various carbon nanomaterials, including single-wall carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide nanoparticles, carbon nanospheres, fullerenes, or carbon materials derived from metal-organic frameworks, have been explored for their non-metallic POD-mimetic enzymatic functions in biosensing, antibacterial applications, and tumor therapy. 28
,
29
,
30 However, the utilization of larger-sized carbon-based materials has encountered challenges such as difficulty in metabolizing, potential mechanical cell damage, and relatively low POD-like activity, limiting their further development as nanocatalytic agents. 31
,
32 In contrast, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are zero-dimensional derivatives of graphene typically measuring less than 10 nm in diameter, not only inherit the outstanding optical and structural properties of graphene but also surpass it in terms of POD-mimic activity, attributed to their excellent electron transport properties and small size effects. 33
,
34
,
35 Nonetheless, the current POD-mimic performance of GQDs still falls short of metal-based nanozymes. 36
,
37 To enhance the catalytic performance of carbon-based catalysts, heteroatom doping, particularly using boron, nitrogen (N), and phosphorus (P), has emerged as a promising strategy, as the doping-induced charge redistribution can increase substrate adsorption and thus improve enzyme activity. 38
,
39 In particular, diatomic doping utilizing N (higher electronegativity [χ = 3.04]) and P (lower electronegativity [χ = 2.19]) holds potential to further enhance enzymatic activity compared to single heteroatom doping, as the synergistic electron effect can generate highly localized states near the Fermi level. 40
,
41
,
42 Therefore, the rational design of GQDs with diatomic doping represents an effective strategy for the development of safe and efficient POD-mimicking enzymes.

In this study, we introduce N/P co-doped GQDs (NPGQDs) as nanozymes for tumor catalytic therapy (Figure 1 ). These NPGQDs, synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal process, have a primary diameter of 4 nm and are derived from biocompatible erythrocytes as a single precursor. Notably, NPGQDs possess carbon vacancies and exhibit POD-like activity, enabling them to initiate a Fenton-like reaction in the tumor microenvironment (TME) to generate highly cytotoxic ·OH for targeted tumor cell eradication. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations have confirmed that the synergetic effect of N/P co-doping reduces the energy barrier of the POD-like reaction compared to single heteroatom doping, establishing the optimal conditions for enzymatic activity. In in vivo experiments at the final time point, treatment with NPGQDs resulted in a remarkable 77.71% inhibition of tumor growth following intravenous (i.v.) injection, and an even more impressive 93.22% inhibition following intratumor (i.t.) injection, compared to the control treatment using phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). Moreover, the NPGQD treatment demonstrated a significant extension in survival time and showed no discernable toxicity in blood or off-target tissues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structural characterizations of NPGQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
NPGQDs were synthesized using a straightforward one-pot hydrothermal process that employed erythrocytes as a green precursor (Figure 2 A). The erythrocytes from mice, along with their membranes composed of N, P, and carbon (C), served as biocompatible staring materials. Following ultrasound-induced hemolysis, the erythrocyte membranes were fragmented and subsequently carbonized at high temperature, resulting in the formation of NPGQDs with excellent thermal stability as compared to natural enzymes (Figure S1). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging (Figure 2B) demonstrated well-dispersed spherical NPGQDs with an average size of approximately 4.0 nm (Figure S2). The bulk analysis for the hydrodynamic size of NPGQDs using dynamic light scattering (DLS) testing revealed a 15-nm diameter, which is larger than that obtained from the TEM analysis. This size difference can be attributed to the adsorption of water molecules on the hydrophilic surface of NPGQDs in the DLS measurements (Figure S3). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) analysis (Figures 2C and 2D) revealed a lattice spacing of 0.257 nm in NPGQDs, corresponding to the (0 2 0) plane of graphite, confirming the graphene-like structure of the NPGQDs. 43 Notably, numerous vacancy defects were observed within NPGQDs as indicated by dotted curves in Figure 2D. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) imaging (Figure 2E) further revealed that NPGQDs had a thickness of 1.2 nm, equivalent to approximately 2 layers of graphites. 44
,
45 The graphene-like nature of NPGQDs was confirmed by the presence of the characteristic graphite peak at 21.4° in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Figure 2F). 46 The Raman spectrum of NPGQDs exhibited both ordered G-bands (1,582 cm−1) and disordered D-bands (1,378 cm−1), with a G/D intensity ratio of 1.18 (Figure 2G), indicating the presence of a high density of vacancy defects within the electronic structure of NPGQDs. 47
,
48

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and soft X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) techniques were employed to characterize the elemental and electronic states of NPGQDs. Figure 3 A illustrates the composition of NPGQDs, comprising C at 64.28%, oxygen (O) at 22.37%, N at 12.89%, and P at 0.46%. In the C K-edge XANES spectrum (Figure 3B), three prominent absorption features were observed, corresponding to 1s → π∗ (C=C, 284.9 eV), 1s → π∗ (C–N/P–C, 288.2 eV), and 1s → σ∗ (C–C, 292.4 eV) transitions, respectively. 49 These observations are in agreement with the C1s XPS profile (Figure S4A). The N K-edge XANES profile (Figure 3C) and N1s XPS spectrum (Figure S4B) provide evidence for the presence of π∗ pyridinic N, π∗ graphitic N, and σ∗ pyrrolic N bonds in the structure of NPGQDs. 50
,
51 Furthermore, the P modification in the NPGQD skeleton has been confirmed through the P L-edge soft XANES spectrum (Figure 3D). 52 The high-resolution P2p spectrum (Figure 3E) can be deconvoluted into two distinct peaks. The first peak appeared at 133.3 eV and corresponds to P–C bonding, which accounts for 94.33% of the spectrum. The second peak was observed at 134.4 eV and represents P–O bonding, constituting 5.67% of the spectrum. 53 This finding indicates that P doping can lead to an increase in vacancy defects in the carbon skeleton, thereby enhancing the active sites involved in catalytic reaction. 54 The presence of an electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrum signal at g = 2.003 in NPGQDs indicates the existence of unpaired carbon atoms in the carbon skeleton (Figure 3F), which is consistent with the findings from Raman analysis and HRTEM observations. Furthermore, the O 1s spectrum exhibits three distinct peaks when deconvoluted, corresponding to C=O, C–OH, and O=C–O bonds, respectively (Figure S4C), 55
,
56
,
57 supported further by the O K-edge XANES profile (Figure S4D). Functional groups present on NPGQDs were determined by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectrum of NPGQDs revealed a peak at 1,668 cm−1, which corresponds to the stretching mode of C=O in carboxylic acid (Figure S5). Additionally, the peak at 1,408 cm−1 is attributed to the C–O/C–N stretching vibration. 49 Furthermore, the stretching vibration of the P–C state is evident in the FT-IR spectrum by the peak at 1,458 cm−1. 52 The presence of hydrophilic groups on the surface of NPGQDs is indicated by the broad absorption peak at 3,437 cm−1, which contributes to the negative surface charge (Figure S6) and excellent aqueous stability (Figure S7) of NPGQDs, as confirmed by zeta potential measurements and the observation of NPGQD dispersity in PBS and cell culture medium over a 15 day period.

The optical property of NPGQDs was evaluated using UV-visible (UV-vis) adsorption spectroscopy. Figure 3G demonstrates that NPGQDs exhibit an absorbance peak at 265 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π∗ transition of C=O. NPGQDs displayed a prominent emission peak at 367 nm under excitation at a wavelength of 430 nm. Moreover, a distinct blue photoluminescence (PL) was clearly observed when the dispersion of NPGQDs was exposed to UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm (as shown in the inset of Figure 3G). Figure 3H demonstrates that NPGQDs exhibit a characteristic excitation wavelength-dependent PL profile. 58 The PL quantum yield of NPGQDs was determined to be 2.92% using rhodamine B as a standard for comparison. Additionally, the average lifespan of NPGQDs, measured at an emission wavelength of 434 nm, was found to be 3.24 ns (Figure 3I). This value is consistent with the reported lifespan of GQDs. 59 Notably, no significant fluorescence attenuation was observed when NPGQDs were exposed to laser irradiation at 365 nm for 1 h (Figure S8), indicating the excellent photostability of NPGQDs. Furthermore, the imaging capability of NPGQDs was confirmed in vitro using a 4T1 cell line. In PBS-treated cells, no fluorescence signal was detected (Figure S9, top). In contrast, fluorescence emission was observed across different citation channels (blue, green, and red, Figure S9, bottom) when 4T1 cells were treated with NPGQDs, demonstrating the specificity of NPGQDs for cellular imaging and the ability of NPGQDs to elicit fluorescence illumination from the treated cells for multi-color imaging applications. Remarkably, NPGQDs were observed to distribute evenly in the cytoplasm of 4T1 cells while not being present in the nucleus. This finding suggests that NPGQDs are able to traverse the cell membrane, indicating their potential as a promising therapeutic agent for cancer treatment.

The POD-like activity of NPGQDs was examined through the analysis of the catalysis of the Fenton-like reaction (Figure 4 A). The generation of ·OH was detected using an EPR spectrometer, which captured the formation of radical adducts ·OH in the presence of a spin-trap molecule, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO). EPR spectra of DMPO-·OH revealed that NPGQDs catalyzed the production of ·OH specifically under acidic conditions (Figure 4B), indicating the tumor-specific responsiveness of NPGQDs. Further, the intensity of the ·OH signal in the EPR spectra correlates with the concentration of NPGQDs (Figure S10). Subsequently, the POD-like activity of NPGQDs was further assessed using a colorimetric method based on detecting tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). Figure S11 shows that no peak corresponding to blue cationic radicals (oxidized-TMB [oxTMB]) was observed in the absence of H2O2, confirming the specificity of NPGQDs in catalyzing the conversion of H2O2 into ·OH. Conversely, the presence of both NPGQDs and H2O2 in acetic buffer (pH = 6.2) resulted in the observation of oxTMB peaks, confirming the generation of ·OH. These findings demonstrate that NPGQDs possess the ability to selectively recognize the TME and catalyze the Fenton-like reaction.

The production of ·OH exhibited a proportional relationship with both the concentration of H2O2 and the reaction time (Figure S12). The quantification of the ·OH generation rate at different concentrations of H2O2 demonstrated a close fit to the typical Michaelis-Menten curve, with a maximum velocity of 0.247 μM s−1 and a Michaelis-Menten constant of 220.78 mM (Figures 4C and S13A). Notably, the Michaelis-Menten maximum velocity for H2O2 of NPGQDs is 10.5 times higher than that of conventional CQD-based POD mimics (Figure S13B). Furthermore, NPGQDs exhibited similarly remarkable catalytic ability by changing the reaction substrate to TMB (Figures S13C and S13D). In comparison to commercial horseradish peroxidase (HRP), the Michaelis-Menten maximum velocity of NPGQDs is approximately half that of HRP (Figure S14).

In order to investigate the underlying factors contributing to exceptional POD-like performance of NPGQDs, we conducted DFT calculations on one-heteroatom-doping models and co-doping models (Figure 4D; more details about models and computation are shown in the supplemental information) for multiple-step POD-like reactions:
(Equation 1)
H 2
O 2
( aq )
→
∗ OH + ∗ OH
(Equation 2)
∗ OH + ∗ OH
→
∗ O + ∗
H 2
O
(Equation 3)
∗ O + ∗
H 2
O
→
∗ O +
H 2
O
( aq )
(Equation 4)
∗ O +
H 2
O
aq
+
H +
+
e −
→
∗ OH +
H 2
O
aq
(Equation 5)
∗ OH +
H 2
O
( aq )
+
H +
+
e −
→
2
H 2
O
( aq )

The results of DFT calculations indicate that all one-heteroatom-doping configurations, namely the graphitic N-doped graphene (Ng-rGO) model, the pyrrolic N-doped graphene (Np-rGO) model, and the P-doped graphene (P-rGO) model, favor the formation of two adsorbed OH∗ species through the chemisorption of H2O2 (Figures 4E and S15). Among these configurations, the P-rGO model exhibits the most favorable catalytic reaction, with a Gibbs free energy (ΔGOH∗+OH∗) of −1.86 eV. In the case of Ng-rGO, the rate-determining step (ΔGO∗+H 2 O∗ = 0.38 eV) indicates that Ng-rGO has catalytic inertness. Conversely, Np-rGO was thermodynamically favored for the POD-like reaction, as pyrrolic N is located at the edge of carbon skeleton. For the P and pyrrolic N co-doped graphene (PNp-rGO) model, the energy barrier for adsorbed OH∗ (ΔGOH∗+OH∗ = −0.38 eV) is similar to that of Np-rGO when the catalytic active site is at N (PNp-rGO(N)), while the catalytic activity is higher than that of Np-rGO or P-rGO when the active site is at P (PNp-rGO(P); Figures 4E and S16). Of particular interest, the P and graphitic N co-doped graphene (PNg-rGO) model exhibits the highest thermodynamic favorability for the POD-like reaction, as evidenced by the strong absorption of OH∗ (ΔGOH∗+OH∗ = −2.56 eV; Figures 4E and S16). Overall, the synergistic electron effect of graphitic N and P atoms could enhance the enzymatic function of NPGQDs compared to N- or P-doped carbon-based nanozymes alone. 60 The co-doping of heteroatoms in NPGQDs brings about modulation in local charge redistribution, leading to improved adsorption of catalytic substrates and enhanced enzymatic activity. 61
,
62 The precise control of heteroatom co-doping offers a promising strategy for tailoring the catalytic properties of nanomaterials for various applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro induction of apoptosis and ferroptosis by NPGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The potential of NPGQDs as a CDT agent was evaluated in vitro through the induction of cellular apoptosis and ferroptosis. The antiproliferative effect of NPGQDs was investigated using a 4T1 cell line. The cells were treated with varying concentrations of NPGQDs for 24 h, and the viability of the cells was assessed using the CellTiter-Glo luminescent assay under both neutral and acid pH conditions (Figure 5 A). It was observed that the presence of a slightly acidic TME is essential for initiating the Fenton-like response of NPGQDs. Under weak acidic conditions, the effectiveness of NPGQDs in inducing tumor cell death increased with increasing concentrations of NPGQDs. In contrast, the viability of cells remained high (∼100%) and unchanged at neutral pH regardless of the concentration of NPGQDs. The cells were then stained with dead/live regents, and fluorescence images were captured 24 h after incubating cells with NPGQDs at pH 6.2 (Figure 5B) and neutral pH (Figure S17), and the results were consistent with the findings presented in Figure 5A. To evaluate the ability of NPGQDs to generate ·OH in cells, reactive oxygen species (ROS) staining was performed. The intensity of the bright green color increased with increasing concentrations of NPGQDs and reached its maximum when the concentration of NPGQDs was 100 μg mL−1 at acid pH (Figures 5C and S18). In contrast, the signal intensity showed no significant changes with varying concentrations of NPGQDs at neutral pH (Figure S19). These results demonstrate that the inhibitory effect on cell proliferation is attributed to the induction of intracellular oxidative stresses by NPGQDs.

To investigate whether NPGQDs induced oxidative damage, 4T1 cells were pre-treated with antioxidants including ascorbic acid or fat-soluble α-tocopherol, followed by treatment with NPGQDs. The results revealed that cell viability increased with increasing concentrations of ascorbic acid (Figure 5D) or α-tocopherol (Figure 5E), indicating that the nanocatalyst is capable of initiating intracellular Fenton-type Harber-Weiss reactions and lipid peroxidation. This also suggests that the cytotoxic effects of NPGQDs are mediated through oxidative stress pathways. To investigate the underlying mechanisms of cell death, 4T1 cells were pre-incubated with specific inhibitors of apoptosis, necroptosis, autophagy, or ferroptosis for 2 h under a weak acid condition (pH 6.2) and were subsequently treated with NPGQDs for 24 h. The results (Figures 5F and S20) demonstrated that the viability of 4T1 cells pre-incubated with ferrostatin-1 (ferroptosis inhibitor) or Ac-DEVD-CHO (apoptosis inhibitor) was improved. In contrast, the viability of cells pre-incubated with necrostatin-1 (necroptosis inhibitor) or 3-methyladenine (autophagy inhibitor) showed little or no improvement. These findings indicate that the observed cell damage is primarily mediated through apoptosis and ferroptosis pathways.

The potential systemic toxicity of NPGQDs was extensively evaluated through both in vitro and in vivo experiments. In the in vitro study, 4T1 cells and HeLa cells were incubated with NPGQDs at a concentration of 400 μg mL−1, and their viability was assayed after 3 days. The results demonstrated that both cell lines remained a viability above 94%, indicating no significant cytotoxicity induced by NPGQDs (Figure S21). To investigate the biosafety of NPGQDs in vivo, an aggressive mouse model of triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) was utilized. Mice were i.v. administered with NPGQDs and sacrificed at three time points (12, 24, and 36 h) post-injection. Organ samples were collected and evaluated to assess the biodistribution of NPGQDs. It was observed that NPGQDs predominantly accumulated in the clearance organs, such as the liver and kidneys, and gradually cleared from other organs except the tumor site (Figure S22A). Over the course of 36 h, NPGQDs exhibited an increased accumulation at the tumor site, attributed to the enhanced permeability and retention effect, which is advantageous for CDT. Blood samples were collected at various time intervals (2, 8, 15, and 30 min and 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, and 24 h) following tail-vein administration, and the blood half-life of NPGQDs was estimated to be 0.48 h (Figure S22B). To evaluate the potential toxicity of NPGQDs on blood, liver, and other tissues, nude-type mice were i.v. injected with NPGQDs. Blood samples were collected 15 days after injection for comprehensive blood analysis, and the mice were sacrificed for marker gene expression analysis and histopathological examination. A group of mice receiving PBS injections was included as baseline reference. The blood analysis encompassed parameters such as urea levels, red blood cell (RBC) count, white blood cell (WBC) count, alkaline phosphatase (ALP), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), hemoglobin (HGB) concentration, mean corpuscular volume (MCV), and hematocrit (HCT). No discernible differences were observed between the treated group and the control group, indicating no significant adverse effects on blood parameters (Figures S23A–S23C). The impact of NPGQDs on inflammatory and endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress responses in clearance organs (kidney, liver, and spleen) of mice was assessed using reverse-transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) assay. No significant differences were observed in the expression of marker genes between the group treated with NPGQDs and the control group (Figures S23D–S23F). Additionally, histopathological examination of various tissues using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining revealed no significant pathological changes between the NPGQD-treated group and the control group (Figure S24). Collectively, these findings confirm the favorable biocompatibility and biosafety profile of NPGQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo therapeutic assessment of NPGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The therapeutic potential of NPGQDs as a POD-mimic enzyme was evaluated using mice bearing 4T1 tumors following the treatment scheduled shown in Figure 6 A. Once the subcutaneous tumor volume reached about 70 mm3, the mice were randomly assigned to three groups. The control group received an i.v. injection of PBS, the NPGQD (i.v.)-treated group received an i.v. injection of NPGQDs, and the NPGQD (i.t.)-treated group received an i.t. injection of NPGQDs. The tumor volume and body weight of each mouse were monitored daily starting from 1 day post-injection and continued for a period of 2 weeks. The NPGQD-treated groups (both i.v. and i.t.) showed a significant inhibition of tumor growth compared to the PBS-treated group over the 15 day treatment period (Figures 6B and 6C). The body weights of the mice in all treatment groups did not show any noticeable difference, indicating that the injection of NPGQDs did not cause significant adverse side effects (Figure 6D). On day 15 post-injection, the mice were euthanized, and the tumors were collected and weighted to evaluate the treatment efficacy. As shown in Figures 6E and 6F, NPGQD-treated groups exhibited significantly smaller tumor volumes and masses compared to the PBS-treated group. Notably, i.t. injection of NPGQDs resulted in a higher accumulation of NPGQDs at tumor sites compared to i.v. injection, leading to a more pronounced inhibitory effect on tumor growth. The tumor growth inhibition by i.v. injection was 77.71%, while the i.t. injection achieved a remarkable tumor growth inhibition of 93.22% in comparison to mice treated with PBS. Figure 6G presents the survival of mice under different treatment conditions, demonstrating that the NPGQD treatment significantly prolonged overall survival. Mice treated by i.t. injection of NPGQDs exhibited the longest survival, surpassing 50 days.

One set of tumor tissues was subjected to immunostaining for ROS and TUNEL analysis to investigate the mechanism of tumor cell death. Significant tissue damage was observed in the tumors from mice receiving NPGQD treatments (Figure 7 A). Furthermore, NPGQD treatment, particularly via i.t. injection, induced a significant increase in cell apoptosis (Figure 7B). The tumor-suppressor mechanism of NPGQDs as a biocatalyst was analyzed by performing ROS staining on ex vivo tumors obtained from mice treated with NPGQDs 24 h post-injection. The NPGQD (i.t.)-treated group exhibited the highest level of ROS, followed by the NPGQD (i.v.)-treated group, while the PBS-treated group displayed little or no green fluorescence (Figure 7C). Additionally, no discernible ROS expression was observed in the heart, liver, spleen, lung, or kidney (Figure S25), suggesting that the ROS generation induced by NPGQDs was specific to the tumor tissues. The fractional positivity of both TUNEL and ROS staining exhibited an increasing trend in tumor tissues (Figures 7D and 7E), indicating a higher level of cell apoptosis and ROS generation. In contrast, no discernible ROS expression was observed in the heart, liver, spleen, lung, or kidney (Figure S26), suggesting that the generation of ROS by NPGQDs was specific to the tumor tissues. These findings confirm that NPGQDs can effectively serve as a POD-mimicking enzyme, catalyzing the in situ generation of ·OH in tumor tissues, leading to cancer cell death.

We have successfully developed a nanozyme in the form of erythrocyte-membrane-derived GQDs (NPGQDs). These NPGQDs exhibit a spherical shape (∼4.0 nm), demonstrate no detectable off-target toxicity, and possess excellent POD-mimicking function. Notably, their Michaelis-Menten maximum velocity of 0.247 μM s−1, in the presence of H2O2 as a substrate, surpasses that of classical GQDs and graphene oxide by 10-fold. This superior catalytic performance can be attributed to the localized electron cloud rearrangement resulting from diatomic doping interactions. The mechanisms underlying cell death induced by NPGQDs have been extensively investigated, revealing apoptosis and ferroptosis as primary pathways triggered by NPGQDs. These pathways promote the generation of cytotoxic ·OH and lipid peroxidation, particularly under weak acid conditions in the TME, both in vitro and in vivo. NPGQDs exhibit favorable physicochemical properties for in vivo application. They accumulate in tumor tissues through the enhanced permeability and retention effect, effectively eradicating tumor cells by inducing high levels of ROS activity. Importantly, NPGQDs do not induce ROS activity when accumulated in clearance organs at neutral pH. Furthermore, NPGQDs demonstrate no harmful effects to blood components, exhibit no changes in marker gene expression, and do not cause any discernible pathological alternations.

In vivo studies using mice bearing 4T1 tumors showed remarkable therapeutic efficacy of NPGQDs. Treatment with NPGQDs significantly inhibits tumor growth, with i.t. administration leading to a tumor growth inhibition of approximately 93.22%, while i.v. administration achieves a growth inhibition of 77.71% compared to PBS-treated mice. Moreover, NPGQD (i.v.) treatment significantly prolongs the survival of tumor-bearing mice with a 1.52-fold extension in survival compared to PBS-treated mice. Notably, all mice treated via i.t. injection survived beyond day 50. In conclusion, NPGQDs represent a promising drug-free nanocatalyst with potent therapeutic effects and favorable biocompatibility for CDT of cancer. Their unique properties make them an attractive candidate for further development and translation into clinical applications.
Further information and requests for resources and reagents should be directed to and will be fulfilled by the lead contact, Hui Wang (hw39@hmfl.ac.cn).
This study did not generate new unique reagents.
All data supporting the findings of this study are available within the paper and its supplemental information or from the corresponding authors upon reasonable request.

The blood samples were collected from female nude mice (5 weeks old, Beijing Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology, Beijing, China). The blood was centrifuged (3,000 rpm, 10 min) and washed with pre-cooled PBS (pH = 7.4) to obtain pure erythrocytes. Hemolysis of the erythrocytes was achieved using an ultrasound probe at a specific power and time. The mixture was then centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min) to obtain erythrocyte membranes. All operations are carried out at 4°C.

The erythrocyte membranes (10 mg) were dispersed in 20 mL deionized water and sonicated under high power for 20 min. The resulting mixture solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (25 mL, Anhui Kemi Instrument) and maintained at 200°C for 20 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was vigorously sonicated for 15 min and centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 10 min. NPGQDs were obtained by freeze drying the supernatant.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by financial support from the National Key R&D Program of China (grant no. 2021YFA1600202); the fellowship of the China National Postdoctoral Program for Innovative Talents (BX20230111); the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U2032162); the Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation (2308085J18); the Hefei Institutes of Physical Science Director’s Fund (BJPY2021B06); the Collaborative Innovation Program of Hefei Science Center of CAS (2022HSC-CIP013); and the High Magnetic Field Laboratory of Anhui Province (AHHM-FX-2021-04). A portion of this work was supported by the High Magnetic Field Laboratory of Anhui Province. The numerical calculations in this work were performed on the supercomputing system at the Supercomputing Center of the University of Science and Technology of China.

ce:section.ce:para: Conceptualization, H.L. (lead), M.Z. (lead), and H.W. (lead); formal analysis, H.L. (lead), M.Z. (lead), and H.W. (lead); funding acquisition, H.L. (lead) and H.W. (lead); investigation, H.L. (lead), Z.D. (supporting), and Z.Z. (supporting); methodology, H.L. (lead) and H.W. (lead); validation, H.L. (lead) and Z.D. (supporting); visualization, H.L. (lead), M.Z. (lead), and H.W. (lead); writing – original draft, H.L. (lead), M.Z. (lead), and H.W. (lead); writing – review & editing, H.L. (lead), M.Z. (lead), and H.W. (lead); project administration, W.L. (equal), M.Z. (lead), and H.W. (lead); resources, W.L. (equal) and H.W. (lead); supervision, W.L. (equal), M.Z. (lead), and H.W. (lead)
The authors declare no competing interests.
Supplemental information can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matt.2023.12.005.
